tldr: BoxyHQ and Dfns solve different problems — Dfns is MPC wallet-as-a-service, not user authentication. Treat this as adopting a new capability, not a like-for-like migration.
why_switch: Dfns provides MPC-based wallet infrastructure for institutional or fintech use cases
Programmable signing policies and multi-party approval workflows are first-class
HSM-backed key management without running custody operations yourself
Multi-chain coverage suited to crypto/fintech backends
Webhooks and clean APIs fit alongside an existing auth stack
what_to_export: User accounts and profile data (you'll keep these in your existing IdP)
Any wallet or key metadata you currently store yourself (if applicable)
Compliance and KYC data tied to users
Audit logs for any prior signing or transaction history
Your existing SSO configuration (stays with BoxyHQ or another IdP)
Service account tokens and API keys you'll need to retire
export_method: There is no direct export path — Dfns does not replace BoxyHQ Jackson. Keep your existing SSO/identity provider for user authentication, and export only the wallet-related data (if any) you intend to bring under Dfns custody. Coordinate with Dfns' onboarding team for any institutional KYC and compliance prerequisites. Consult Dfns' official documentation for current API specifications.
import_method: Onboard with Dfns through their sales/solutions process given the custom enterprise pricing model. Provision a Dfns environment, configure signing policies and approval rules, and integrate the Dfns SDK or API into your backend. Authentication for end users continues to flow through your existing IdP (BoxyHQ Jackson or its replacement). Tie Dfns wallet identifiers back to your user records via metadata. Run pilots with low-value transactions before raising limits.

concept_mapping.note: Authentication stays with your IdP; Dfns references your user ID
Map your tenancy model into Dfns' resource hierarchy
Move policy enforcement out of your code

concept_mapping.source: API tokens issued by your stack

concept_mapping.target: Dfns activity log + your aggregated logging

concept_mapping.note: These are different controls — model both layers

gotchas: This is not a like-for-like migration — Dfns does not replace user authentication
Pricing is custom enterprise; budget for sales cycle and contract negotiation
Compliance work (KYC, AML, SOC 2 alignment) remains your responsibility
Vendor lock-in on key shares and policy models — exit plan is non-trivial
Fewer end-user UX primitives than consumer-facing wallet SDKs
Plan canary rollouts with low transaction limits before production cutover

email.subject: Adding Dfns wallet infrastructure alongside our auth stack
email.body:
Hi team,

Quick note: Dfns isn't a replacement for BoxyHQ — it's a separate capability for MPC wallet infrastructure. Auth would continue to live in our current IdP; Dfns would handle programmable custody.

Plan if we proceed:
1. Engage Dfns sales for pricing and compliance scope.
2. Spike the SDK integration in a sandbox environment.
3. Define signing policies and approval workflows.
4. Pilot with low-limit transactions before scaling.

Effort estimate: 3-8 weeks given the procurement and compliance overhead. Open questions: custody model, regulatory scope, and how Dfns user references map to our existing accounts.

Thanks
